summary: High‑energy Greensboro show with soaring Morning Dew and killer Terrapin jam
blurb: The first set opens strong and builds into a powerful second‑set climax featuring Hey Pocky Way, Terrapin Station and an unforgettable post‑Terrapin jam. Jerry’s scorching solo on Morning Dew and Brent’s vibrant keyboards drive the performance. The night is a must‑listen for fans of the late‑80s era.
review: On March 31, 1989 at the Greensboro Coliseum the Dead delivered a high‑octane set that still feels fresh decades later. The first set rolls through classics like New Minglewood Blues and Loser before slipping into a brief dip on Victim Or The Crime, but the band quickly recovers. The second set is where the magic happens – Hey Pocky Way erupts with Brent’s exuberant piano work, leading straight into a seamless Terrapin Station that blossoms into a thunderous post‑Terrapin jam. Mickey Hart’s drumming practically jumps into the crowd, and Jerry Garcia lights up the night with a scorching Morning Dew solo that many reviewers call “on fire.” The encore closes on a soaring Brokedown Palace, leaving the audience buzzing. Across all available recordings the show is praised for its energy, tight interplay, and especially the standout moments of Terrapin Station, Morning Dew, and Brent’s keyboard brilliance, making this one of the late‑80s highlights in the Dead’s catalog.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Three recordings are clearly positive and one is mixed; reviewers repeatedly praise the Morning Dew solo, Terrapin jam and Brent's keyboards, yielding strong positive sentiment across the majority of sources.

best_recording.reason: Solid soundboard source with crisp overall balance, captures the high‑energy performance and highlights like Hey Pocky Way and Morning Dew without major audio glitches.

key_highlights: High‑energy second set climax featuring Terrapin Station and an unforgettable post‑Terrapin jam
Jerry’s scorching solo on Morning Dew that many describe as “on fire”
Brent Mydland’s vibrant keyboards on Hey Pocky Way and I Will Take You Home
Mickey Hart’s drum work during the Terrapin jam, even pointing sticks at fans

band_performance.Jerry: Scorching solos on Morning Dew and high‑energy playing throughout, with a few mixed comments on the late‑set solo but overall praised for his fire.
band_performance.Phil: Bass bombs on Morning Dew earned specific praise; otherwise no notable remarks.
band_performance.Brent: Keyboard work shines – vibrant piano on Hey Pocky Way, beautiful playing on I Will Take You Home, and a memorable solo that fans miss after his era.
band_performance.Mickey: Energizing drumming, especially during the Terrapin jam where he pointed sticks at fans; compared favorably to Rush’s “Passage to Bangkok”.
band_performance.Billy: Solid, predictable percussion that underpins the set without drawing negative criticism.
